skill.name: frontend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Guidelines for building React and TypeScript frontends with Suspense, lazy loading, TanStack Query, TanStack Router, MUI v7, feature folders, and performance patterns.
skill.summary: Apply consistent React and TypeScript frontend patterns for modern app development.
skill.author: diet103

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but AI review found documentation false positives in Markdown and TypeScript examples. Backticks are code formatting, dynamic imports are React lazy loading, relative paths are import examples, and network references describe normal frontend API clients. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malicious command execution, or executable installer behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Markers Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The static analyzer matched Markdown backticks and TypeScript examples as Ruby or shell execution. Reviewed locations are documentation snippets, not executable skill code or command runners.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced or inline Markdown examples. No backtick shell execution context, Ruby code, or command invocation was found in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: React Dynamic Imports Misclassified as Script Risk
low_findings.description: Dynamic import() appears in React.lazy and performance examples for code splitting. This is normal frontend bundler behavior and does not load attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The module specifiers are static strings in frontend documentation examples. I found no user-controlled import path or obfuscated loader behavior.

low_findings.title: Relative Import Paths Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../../ and ../ sequences are links or TypeScript import examples that teach project organization. They are not file reads, writes, archive extraction, or runtime path resolution against user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show documentation links and import declarations. No filesystem API usage or user-supplied path concatenation was present.

low_findings.title: Frontend API Client Guidance Misclassified as Network Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The network matches describe ordinary application API access through a configured apiClient and one abortable fetch example. No external collection endpoint, secret transmission, or credential harvesting pattern was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are generic frontend data-fetching guidance. The only fetch path reviewed is a relative application path, and there is no evidence of exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: Scanner Blocker Terms Are Benign Documentation Text
low_findings.description: Several blocker detections appear to come from words used in frontend guidance, such as critical UX rules, includes(), password field examples, and passwordHash type examples. These do not indicate weak cryptography or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible documentation or UI example code. I found no cryptographic implementation, hashing routine, system probe, or host reconnaissance command.

content.user_title: Build React Frontends with Modern Patterns
content.value_statement: React teams often lose consistency across components, routing, data fetching, and styling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical frontend rules for predictable implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides React component structure with TypeScript props, hooks, handlers, render sections, and exports.
Recommends Suspense-based loading with React.lazy and useSuspenseQuery for new components.
Defines feature folder organization for APIs, components, hooks, helpers, types, and public exports.
Explains TanStack Router patterns for folder-based routes, lazy route components, and loaders.
Provides MUI v7 styling guidance, including sx usage, Grid syntax, and style file boundaries.
Covers performance practices such as memoization, dynamic imports, debouncing, and cleanup.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not execute code, install dependencies, or inspect a live project by itself.
Examples assume a React, TypeScript, MUI, TanStack Query, and TanStack Router stack.
Project-specific aliases such as @/, ~types, ~components, and ~features must already be configured.
It does not replace accessibility, security, or product-specific design review.

use_cases.title: Create Consistent Components
use_cases.description: Use the skill to structure new React components with TypeScript props, Suspense loading, stable handlers, and clear exports.

use_cases.title: Standardize Feature Work
use_cases.description: Use the skill to align feature folders, API services, route files, hooks, helpers, and shared type boundaries.

use_cases.title: Review Frontend Changes
use_cases.description: Use the skill to check pull requests for loading states, routing patterns, MUI styling, performance issues, and TypeScript quality.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend development guidelines to create a React TypeScript component for [feature]. Include props, Suspense loading guidance, MUI layout, and export style.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Feature Folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend development guidelines to plan the file structure for a new [feature] feature. Include API, components, hooks, helpers, types, and route placement.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Data Fetching
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React data fetching code with the frontend development guidelines. Recommend changes for useSuspenseQuery, cache-first behavior, error handling, and loading boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Frontend Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this frontend module against the guidelines. Check component structure, routing, API service boundaries, MUI v7 styling, performance, TypeScript usage, and anti-patterns.

output_examples.input: Create a user profile page using the skill.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a feature folder, a lazy route component, a Suspense boundary, a typed API service, and MUI layout guidance.

output_examples.input: Review a component with manual loading checks.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends replacing early loading returns with SuspenseLoader, moving data access into useSuspenseQuery, and preserving layout stability.
The assistant suggests loading heavy export libraries only when the user starts the export and keeping the primary page bundle smaller.

best_practices: Use the skill before creating new frontend files so structure, imports, and loading behavior are consistent from the start.
Provide the existing project stack and aliases when asking for implementation guidance.
Ask for review against one topic at a time when you need precise feedback.
anti_patterns: Do not apply the examples blindly to projects that do not use React, TypeScript, MUI, or TanStack libraries.
Do not use the skill as a substitute for running project tests, type checks, and accessibility checks.
Do not copy placeholder routes, types, or API names without adapting them to the real application.

faq.question: Does this skill write frontend code?
faq.answer: It guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code when they write or review frontend code. The skill itself is documentation.

faq.question: Which frontend stack does it target?
faq.answer: It targets React with TypeScript, MUI v7, TanStack Query, TanStack Router, and Suspense-based loading patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with existing legacy components?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify modernization steps, but legacy constraints may require gradual adoption.

faq.question: Does it include styling guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers MUI sx usage, separate style files, Grid syntax, responsive layout, and theme-aware styling.

faq.question: Does it handle backend API design?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on frontend API service layers and client-side data fetching patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to install?
faq.answer: The reviewed files are Markdown documentation and examples. No executable installer, malware behavior, or prompt injection was found.
